Code fragment: public final TableField<NetworkPlacesRecord, UUID> NETWORK_PLACE_ID = createField(DSL.name("network_place_id"), SQLDataType.UUID.nullable(false).defaultValue(DSL.field(DSL.raw("gen_random_uuid()"), SQLDataType.UUID)), this, "");

Code fragment: public final TableField<NetworkRouteStopPointsRecord, Integer> NETWORK_ROUTE_STOP_POINT_REGULATED_TIMING_POINT_STATUS = createField(DSL.name("network_route_stop_point_regulated_timing_point_status"), SQLDataType.INTEGER.nullable(false).defaultValue(DSL.field(DSL.raw("'-9999'::integer"), SQLDataType.INTEGER)), this, "");
Explanation: Using -9999 as a default value to represent a missing or unknown regulated timing point status is problematic because it conflates a magic number with actual data, making queries error-prone and requiring special handling. Consider making this column NULLABLE and using NULL to represent unknown values, or create a lookup table with explicit status codes including an 'unknown' entry.

Using -9999 as a default value to represent missing data is problematic because it's a magic number that could be confused with legitimate data and requires special handling throughout the application. Consider making this column NULLABLE and using NULL to represent missing values, or use a separate boolean column to indicate whether the value is present.

The column uses a magic value '-9999' as a default to represent missing data instead of using NULL. Consider making the column nullable and using NULL to represent missing values, or document the semantic meaning if -9999 has a legitimate business meaning.
